content.full_text:
STATE OF CAI.IFORNIA
PETE wll-soN, Gov.r,'or
PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION
505 VAN NESS AVTNUE
sAN FRANCTSCO, CA 91102-3298
JL)
June 19, 19 92 
,r
fair PoIitical Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0814
Dear Madam or Sir:
"92
Re: Request for Formal Written Advice Relating to Out-of-Country
Ins pection
This letter is a request for formaf written adwice pursuant to
covernment Code section 83114(b) and FPPC Regulation 18329. I am
an attorney with the California Public Utilities 
Commission
(CPUC) and am responsible for responding to conflict of interest
inquiries within our agency, I have been authorized to send this
request on behalf of Tinothy Kenney and }lark Bumgardner. Both
Mr. Kenney and !1r. Bumgardner are designated employees of the
CPUC. Mr. Kenney is a Principal- FinanciaL Examiner, and Mr.
Bumgardner is a Financial Examiner Iv. 
The nailing address for
both employees is: 
California Public UtiLities Commission, 505
van Ness Avenue, San Francisco, California 94102.
Bac kqround
The CPUC intends to perform an inspection of the books and
records of Pacific Gas and Electric Company's (PG&E's) Canadian
subsidiaries and affiliates 
to determine if PG&E's purchases of
Canadian gas by long-term contracts are reasonable. This tl?e of
review is an integraf part of the CPUC's responsibility for
ensuring that public utility 
rates are just and reasonable. The
inspection is being performed pursuant to PubLic Utiliuies 
Code
seclion 314(b), which provides that the CPUC may inspect the
accounts, books, papers, and documents of any subsidiary or
af f iLj-ate of a public utility'
The CPUC has assigned Mr, Kenney and Mr. Bumgardner to Perform
the initiaL phase of the inspection. !Ir. Kenney and Mr.
Bumgardner plan to travef to Calgary, Canada on June 22, 1992 and
to iemain there until JuIy 3, 1992 for this purpose. The CPUC
intends to reimburse Mr. Kenney and Mr. Bumgardner for the travel
expenses they incur in connection with the trip to Canada. In
order to obtain reinbursement for out-of-country travel , the
state finance office has to approve of the trip. 
The CPUC wi11,
in turn, request reimbursement from PG&E for the costs of
airfare, lodging, and car rental associated rrith the trip.
nO?


J


' Fair Political Practices Commission
June 19, 1992
Page 2
It is rrithin the 6ole discretion of the CPUC to determine which
employees are assigned to this inspection. In addition, the CPUC
exercises substantial control over the use of any reimbursement
. provided by Pc&E. (Cf . Stone Opinion, 3 FPPC 52, 57 (No. 77-003,
June 7, 1977\.\
Issue
fhe question presented to you is whether any of the travel
payments are considered "gifts" or "income" to the individual
employees under the PoliticaL Reform Act?
I have obtained oraL advice from Blanca Breeze, an attorney with
the FPPC, which indicates that any reimbursement by PG&E Lo the
CPUC hToul,d not be considered a gift or income to the individual
employees under these circumstances. I believe that this advice
is based on the provision of the Government Code which states
that "income" does not include reimbursement for expenses
received frorn a state agency. (Gov. Code S 82030(b)(2).) This
is consistent with advice given pursuant to a request for
informal assistance, which concluded that CPUC reinbursement for
travel expenses incurred by an employee to attend an out-of-state
conference was not a gift or income to the individual, even
though the CPUC, in turn, obtained reimbursement from the
conference sponsor. (See !'ogelman Request for Informal
Assistance, No. I-90-618 (January 11, 1991).)
Please call- me at (415) 703-2053 if you need any further
information regarding this request for advice.
Sincerely,
9r"o.o Att-'-'--
(ffdith A1len
Public Utilities 
Counsel-
JA: jmc


\/

sections.question: The question presented to you is whether any of the travel payments are considered "gifts" or "income" to the individual employees under the Political Reform Act?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether reimbursement by PG&E to the CPUC for travel expenses incurred by designated employees inspecting PG&E's Canadian subsidiaries constitutes taxable income or gifts under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from CPUC to FPPC, not an FPPC response letter. The document cites prior oral advice from FPPC attorney Blanca Breeze and a prior informal assistance request (Vogelman) but does not contain an FPPC conclusion section. The letter references Background and Issue sections but lacks formal Question and Conclusion formatting typical of FPPC advice letters.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The question presented to you is whether any of the travel payments are considered "gifts" or "income" to the individual employees under the Political Reform Act?

embedding.summary: The California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) requests formal written advice regarding whether travel expense reimbursements from PG&E for two designated employees' inspection trip to Canada should be considered gifts or income under the Political Reform Act.
